Syria's Battle on All Fronts
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Two months ago, the carnage was on the streets of Israeli cities, where suicide attacks by fundamentalist Palestinians left more than 60 dead and hundreds wounded. This month, it has been in southern Lebanon, where Israeli bombing -- retaliation for rocket attacks by Hezbollah fighters -- has killed at least 100 civilians and displaced hundreds of thousands. 

     In dealing with the man who can end this violence -- President Hafez al-Assad of Syria -- it's important to understand that both sets of attacks on Israel are part of an effort to disrupt the Arab-Israeli peace process. Yet the United States treats Mr. Assad as a peacemaker, sending Secretary of State Warren Christopher to Damascus, hat in hand, to ask for help. (Competing with six other foreign ministers, Mr. Christopher was kept waiting over the weekend while Mr. Assad met first with Foreign Minister Yevgeny M. Primakov of Russia.)

A HUD Strikeout
nytimes_articles.abstract:
A woman I will call Mrs. Hardy arrived at Bronx Legal Services at the 11th hour. Her story was a familiar one. Her landlord wanted to evict her because her estranged crack-addicted husband had been arrested for a drug offense just blocks away from her city-owned apartment. Although he had been drifting through New York City shelters for two years, he gave the police his wife's address as his residence. Later, he confessed he had done so "out of spite." 

     When he pleaded guilty, the city moved to evict Mrs. Hardy. She was unable to convince the court that her husband no longer lived with her.

Justice For All, Twice
nytimes_articles.abstract:
It took two trials to do justice in the serial story of Bernhard Goetz. 

     The first trial, the six-week criminal hearing in 1987, focused on whether Mr. Goetz overreacted after four black teen-agers surrounded him on a downtown No. 2 train and asked him for money. It was Mr. Goetz against the group his lawyers called "the predators" and "the gang of four."

Beef, Beef, Everywhere, But Hardly a Nibble to Eat
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I KNOW how to cook three things: chili with ground beef, spaghetti with ground beef, and chicken. Thanks to the mad cow scare in Britain, it's down to chicken. 

     But the chicken tastes like fish, because that's what they feed the chickens  here. The chickens eat fish, the fish eat beef, the beef eat lamb. A food chain  gone awry.

nytimes_articles.headline: Cumin and Coriander: Exotic Spices With Familiar Faces
nytimes_articles.abstract:
CUMIN and coriander are like the Burns and Allen of spices -- approachable, easy to like and always entertaining. 

     But unlike cooks in tropical countries, most Americans did not grow up selecting wares from the local spice vendor as a part of daily shopping. Instead, cooks here tend to think of the aromatic barks, flowers, buds, roots and seeds that comprise the spice lexicon as exotic and rather foreign. Consequently, spices, while enticing, can also be intimidating.

wiki_events.headline: In the United States, the Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996 is passed into law.

pages.title: Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996
pages.abstract: The Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996 (AEDPA), Pub. L. 104–132 (text) (PDF), 110 Stat. 1214, enacted April 24, 1996, was introduced to the United States Congress in April 1995 as a Senate Bill. The bill was passed with broad bipartisan support by Congress in response to the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing. It was signed into law by President Bill Clinton.
